data.title: SiTime: The Only Pure-Play MEMS Story In Semis, But Waiting For A Better Entry
data.description:
Introduction

SiTime's (SITM) shares have experienced an amazing run-up since last year; the stock is up more than 100% over this 1-year period, driven by investor enthusiasm over its growth prospects. Currently, SiTime's shares have experienced a slight pullback of 26% since their highs of $440 per share in early March. This is on the back of an industry-wide tech stock selloff, which was induced by the US-Iran war. You might be wondering whether this could be a potential entry point into the stock; however, I am initiating the stock with a HOLD recommendation, which is premised on a few key reasons:

First, SiTime is benefitting from AI-related growth as data centers are adopting its MEMS oscillators in both AI servers and networking equipment. The company has also secured a major design win in Apple's (AAPL) C1 internal modem, which could potentially drive large shipment growth for the company as Apple gradually replaces Qualcomm's (QCOM) modem with its own internal design. But looking at the company's expensive valuation of 60x FY2026 P/E, I prefer waiting for a further pullback in stock prices before recommending a BUY.

Business Overview

SiTime is a provider of precision timing components for the global electronics industry. Currently, most of the timing components used in the world are based on quartz resonators/oscillators, as it is a mature and low-cost technology for timekeeping. The key differentiator of SiTime is that they provide silicon-based MEMS timing components, which is a relatively new technology that is disrupting the time-keeping market. Compared to quartz, MEMS-based resonator/oscillators offer better reliability - traditional quartz resonators/oscillators are prone to vibration or shock damage, which means they are much more fragile. Besides that, quartz resonators/oscillators have higher power consumption and occupy a larger space compared to MEMS. The main drawback for MEMS, however, is its higher cost, which is why it is not widely adopted yet today.

But currently, we are at a key inflection point for the timing components - we are reaching the point where customers are getting less concerned about the cost of MEMS and more concerned about its benefits. Today, SiTime's MEMS solutions are being used in more and more applications around us, such as consumer electronics, enterprise, automotive, industrial, defence and even AI systems and data centres. SiTime has been rapidly growing over the past few years; since its IPO in 2019, the company has roughly 4x in size, from $84 million to $327 million in 2025. The future is bright for SiTime, and the company has captured less than 10% of its SAM, which indicates a long growth runway.

CED Growth Driven by AI Server Demand

The major driver for SiTime will be its communications, enterprise & data center segment (CED). The growth of this segment has greatly outpaced the growth of the overall group - in Q4 FY2025, overall revenue grew by 66%, while CED grew by 160%. The revenue mix of SiTime has shifted drastically; in the past, the company's products were mainly used by IoT/consumer devices. But now, its MEMS components are used in both server networking and AI servers as standard timing solutions.

Figure 1: SiTime's Revenue Mix Comparison

SiTime's components are used across many major areas in the AI ecosystem, such as the CPU board, ToR switches, optical transceivers, and even AECs. The primary product of SiTime is its Elite and Chorus Family, which have ASPs ranging from $3 to $12. Yes, the ASP of SiTime's products is small, which may cause you to underestimate the company. But since they are used in a wide variety of products across the data center, the sheer volume turns it into an attractive market, with a more than $1.2 billion SAM. Since the ASP of MEMS components is small relative to the AI server (which usually costs millions), there is less concern about the high cost of MEMS, as they occupy only a small portion of the server's BoM.

SiTime will be able to ride the megatrend for AI, as the company is a supplier for Nvidia servers. Heading into 2026, notable companies in the supply chain are predicting a doubling in total AI server rack shipments. As AI clusters get larger, there will be a need for both larger networking infrastructure and AI server installations, which will be beneficial to SiTime. Over the next few years, the networking industry will also be progressing to 1.6T switches, which management has guided for a double-digit increase in ASP compared to the ASP in 800G networking switches.

Figure 2: SiTime Product Application in AI Data Centers

Major Design Win in Apple's C1 Modem

Besides data centers, SiTime will benefit from the recent adoption of internal modems in iPhones by Apple. SiTime has been a long-time supplier to Apple for its non-iPhone products, such as the Apple Pencil. Apple has been trying for many years to replace Qualcomm's modems without much progress; the company struggled initially but has finally managed to develop its internally developed C1 5G modem. This modem has been adopted for Apple's iPhone 16e, which signals a turning point for SiTime's consumer/mobile business. SiTime will be able to ride on this trend, as SiTime is the exclusive MEMS oscillator supplier for Apple's internal modem.

The implications for SiTime are important, as it is set to see its iPhone-related revenue go from virtually nothing to many millions of dollars in the next few years. Analysts are expecting each modem to be paired with two SiTime MEMS oscillators, which translates to about $1 of content per device. This may again seem small, but as Apple ships more than 240 million iPhones a year, the TAM is huge for SiTime. The only question will be the adoption rate of internal modems for iPhones, as not all the 240 million shipments are equipped with modems. Currently, only Apple's iPhone 17 Air and iPhone 16e are equipped with Apple's internal modems, so the adoption rate is relatively low. But there is significant room for growth as more and more of Apple's lineup adopts this internal modem design.

Figure 3: Apple's First In-House C1 Modem

Key Acquisition of a Timing Business Competitor

Data centers and the Apple win have been large drivers of SiTime's stock price over the past year. But the most major recent development for the company will be its acquisition of Renesas's (TYO: 6723) timing business, which was only just announced in February. Although SiTime has acquired companies in the past (such as Aura), the Renesas acquisition will be the company's largest acquisition to date and will put SiTime on the path to achieving its long-term $1 billion revenue target.

The most obvious benefit from this acquisition will be in growing SiTime's CED business - 75% of revenue generated by Renesas's timing business is CED-related. Therefore, management expects to double CED revenue when the Renesas timing business is consolidated into the group. We still have a long way to go until the deal is closed, as it is only expected to happen by the end of this year. The acquisition will also boost SiTime's competitive advantage - SiTime is well-positioned in MEMS-based resonators and oscillators but lacked in terms of clock IC technology. This is exactly where Renesas's timing business excels, which is why the acquisition will greatly strengthen SiTime's competitive position.

Figure 4: SiTime Announcement of Renesas Timing Business Acquisition

Financials

The key concern about SiTime's financials post-acquisition will be the impact on its financials. First, starting with SiTime's top-line - with the incremental $300 million in revenue from Renesas, SiTime's revenue would scale to $327 million (existing revenue in 2025) + $300 million = $627 million, which would be nearly double in size post-acquisition. In terms of margins, the acquisition will be accretive to the group's gross margins; the acquired business has higher gross margins at 70% compared to SiTime's current non-GAAP gross margins of 59% for FY2025. This change will allow SiTime to move closer to its long-term margin target of 60-65%.

You might be wondering, how is SiTime going to finance this acquisition? Based on management's current plan, the company is planning to fund the acquisition with $1.5 billion in cash and 4.13 million in new shares. Management does not have enough cash on hand currently, and the cash portion would be partially funded by a $900 million debt facility, with the remainder financed by the cash on hand. The debt portion is not the primary concern, as management is aiming to reduce the group's overall leverage to under 2x within 24 months of closing the deal. The main concern to shareholders, however, would be the dilution impact, which would represent a 16% dilution based on the current 26.3 million shares outstanding.

Personally, I think that this is a great deal for SiTime, as it can practically double its business at the cost of a reasonable 16% dilution. To Renesas, the company isn't getting the short end of the stick either - the timing business is a small part of Renesas's overall revenue; by divesting its business to SiTime, SiTime will be able to pay better attention to the business and grow the synergised entity's revenue faster than Renesas could originally.

Valuation

The main issue for me currently would be SiTime's valuation. The company's fundamentals are attractive, but the stock is trading at an expensive 60x FY2026 P/E based on consensus estimates; this indicates that investors are already factoring in the structural growth of the business and have already assigned a premium valuation in return. Furthermore, the company's CED opportunity and Apple business have already been known to the market for several months and have already been baked into consensus estimates. A potential upside could be the Renesas acquisition, which I view to be beneficial and is yet to be factored into consensus numbers. But the dilution concern could cause short-term pressures on the stock, which is why I would prefer waiting on the sidelines at the moment.

Figure 5: Consensus Estimates for SiTime (FY2026) (Data by S&P Global)

SiTime is currently the only pure-play MEMS timing solutions provider listed publicly; hence, there are no direct competitors we can compare SiTime with. But there are several timing/analog component companies that we can use as a comparison for SiTime. Compared to its peers, SiTime is clearly trading at a large premium, as its peers are trading at around 27-49x FY2026 P/E. I think it is reasonable for SiTime to be trading at a more expensive level compared to the industry, as they are still small and have a longer growth runway. But the main concern putting me off is the company's absolute valuation.

Peer Analysis of SiTime and Key Metrics, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Company Market Cap (USD) EPS (USD) P/E (2026) EPS Growth (2026)
SiTime 8,600 million 5.2 59.6 62.1%
Microchip Technology 33,600 million 1.6 37.8 19.8%
MPWR 51,700 million 21.5 48.7 21.1%
Analog Devices 150,000 million 11.4 26.8 46.7%
Texas Instrument 173,000 million 6.5 29.6 15.5%

Risk to the Company

There are several key risks that could materially affect the HOLD thesis. First, the main risk for SiTime would be a potential slowdown in AI capex. SiTime is highly reliant on the continuance of AI spending to drive the growth in its CED segment. If there is a material slowdown in hyperscaler AI capex, this could have a material negative impact on the stock. The second would be that SiTime is reliant on the growing adoption of MEMS for growth, which is a more expensive technology compared to quartz. If there is reluctance for customers to adopt MEMS over quartz resonators/oscillators, it could pose a material downside to the stock. Lastly, there is financial risk from the acquisition of the Renesas timing business, which is expected to increase the debt levels of the company. If SiTime fails to create meaningful synergies or integrate Renesas's timing business, it could pose downside risk to the stock.

Conclusion

Looking at the growth trajectory of the company, it seems that management is poised to achieve its $1 billion revenue target soon. As highlighted earlier, the company is on track to nearly double its revenue to $627 million when the Renesas acquisition closes. Factoring in the Apple growth opportunity, if Apple is able to replace 100% of its Qualcomm modems with its own internally developed modem, that alone would create an incremental $200 million in revenue for the company, which brings the total to $827 million. We have also yet to consider the organic growth from SiTime's existing CED business. Clearly, it is quite feasible for management to achieve this target. But with the current market cap of $8 billion and a forward FY2026 P/E of 60x, the market seems to already be pricing in this growth. Hence, I am initiating the stock with a 'Hold' rating and awaiting a further pullback for a potential buying opportunity.

data.title: 2 Royalty Income Streams You Don't Have To Work For
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

You've had those days when you start early, jump straight into solving problems, putting out fires, and juggling deadlines, and before you know it, it's dark outside. It was no doubt a productive day; you got things done, but you didn't really get a break. You didn't recharge. The day just disappeared. You can't even remember what you had for lunch.

That's adulthood for most of us. We stay busy, push ourselves towards career growth, and chase the next big project, the next big raise, and the next promotion. You are thirsty for more, and it matters very much, but it also means our income is tied to our time and our constant involvement. Both of these are finite.

Imagine if some part of your effort kept paying you even when you weren't actively working. Like collecting a small fee every time a piece of code you wrote was run or earning a cut each time a design you created was produced. What if the work you did once produces rewards that keep flowing?

That's the simple power behind royalties. Musicians, actors, and other artists often retain royalty rights to their content and get paid whenever their work is featured. While you can't patent most of your output at work, you can tap into the power of royalties. It's not about doing more. It's about owning streams that continue to pay, even when your day is already full.

Let's now review our top royalty picks for March.

Pick #1: KRP - Yields 9.8%

Kimbell Royalty Partners LP (KRP) holds mineral royalty interests in 17 million gross acres across 28 states, spanning key energy basins in the country. The royalty firm ended December 2025 with 85 rigs actively drilling on its acreage, and this represents 16% of all land rigs drilling in the United States. The company also declared a $0.37/share distribution for Q4, in line with our expectations, and noted that they expect 100% of the payout to be considered Return of Capital for tax purposes. This distribution will be paid on March 25th to shareholders on record on March 18, 2026.

Note: KRP is a C-corp for tax purposes and issues a 1099-DIV to shareholders.

The partnership reported a run-rate average daily production of 25,627 BOE/d, slightly above their guidance. Looking at the bigger picture, KRP has consistently grown its run-rate average daily production levels since its IPO, despite volatile commodity prices. Source

The Permian Basin continued to be the largest cash cow for KRP, representing 51% of revenues during the fourth quarter, followed by the mid-continental and Haynesville regions.

KRP uses debt and active hedging to mitigate commodity price volatility, with ~15% of production hedged on a two-year rolling basis. Speaking about debt, the company has been distributing 75% of its cash available for distribution to shareholders and retaining 25% to pay down debt. In 2025, the partnership paid down $57.4 million of its credit facility and amended and extended it through December 2030. Management noted that they did this and modestly lowered their borrowing costs by 0.35%. KRP ended 2025 with $441.5 million in debt, representing 1.5x TTM Adj. EBITDA.

During the Q4 conference call, management noted that all the partnership's acreage is leased, and advancements in E&P technology will be a strong tailwind for royalty firms, with zero CapEx on their side.

Operators that are on our properties are some of the most innovative people in America, and they're just constantly looking for ways to improve production, whether that's enhancing production techniques or drilling at different depths and different formations. And the good news is that as a mineral owner, we don't have to pay for any of that experimentation or proving out the best areas in which to apply CapEx. So it's going to be a nice windfall for us, and we believe in it. - Davis Ravnaas, CFO

KRP is a strong steward for capital return, and the company's board recently authorized a $100 million share repurchase program through December 2027. Looking ahead, management projects a daily production rate between 24,000 and 27,000 Boe/d, consistent with 2025 numbers (midpoint at 25,500 Boe/d).

The above estimations were provided by management before the military conflict in the Middle East. Global dynamics around crude oil have shifted considerably since, and we expect WTI to remain above $80, indicating strong prospects for annual distributions above $1.60/share, calculating to 9.8% yields at current share prices.

Pick #2: XOMA Preferreds - Up To 8.4% Yields

Xoma Royalty Corporation (XOMA) is a biotech royalty aggregator. In other words, they provide non-dilutive, non-debt, non-recourse funding to biotech companies towards their early-to-mid-stage clinical assets (pre-commercial and commercial stage) in exchange for economic rights to future milestones and royalty payments on eventual product revenues.

In 2017, XOMA transformed from a CapEx- and R&D-heavy biotech company into a royalty aggregator. In 2025, XOMA made 22 portfolio additions, ending the year with 7 commercial and 14 phase-3 assets. It is important to note that XOMA earns not only from royalties on commercial product sales but also from milestone payments triggered as an asset progresses through clinical development or reaches commercial achievements. Royalties are often structured to be in the low to mid single digits. Take, for example, XOMA's 0.5% royalty interest in Vabysmo (Roche), an intravitreal injection used to treat renal diseases like age-related macular degeneration, diabetic macular edema, and retinal vein occlusion. The royalty interest % may seem small, but peak sales for this drug are expected to be over $8 billion, indicating that XOMA stands to collect up to $40 million annually from this drug. Source

XOMA generated positive cash flow from operations in 2025, with over $50 million in portfolio receipts, with two-thirds from royalty payments and the rest from milestone payments. The chart below demonstrates the company's trajectory for cash generation.

There are at least three major data announcements expected in 2026, implying more milestone payments ahead. XOMA ended 2025 with over 120 assets (up from 60 at the end of 2023) and with $83 million in cash on hand.

During 2025, XOMA made $16 million in share repurchases while spending $5.4 million on preferred dividends. The company doesn't pay any dividends on its common stock but has two perpetual preferreds.

  • 8.625% Series A, Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (XOMAP) - Yield 8.2%

  • 8.375% Series B, Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (XOMAO) - Yield 8.4%

XOMA preferreds pay qualified dividends and trade post their call dates (for over four years). Despite their call risk and modest premium to par pricing, these preferreds present attractive income potential at current prices, supported by a unique, rapidly expanding royalty-aggregation model that is poised to generate substantial future cash flows.

Conclusion

At High Dividend Opportunities, we insist that income should come from multiple, reliable sources and not depend on how many hours you can work. The goal is to build a portfolio that sends you paychecks whether you're tackling a busy day or taking a well-deserved vacation with family. We buy investments to create royalties for participating in the financial markets. Ongoing market activity generates reliable income without demanding our constant time and effort.

Your time is finite, but your income doesn't have to be. When your portfolio is filled with durable, royalty-like cash streams, you stop trading your time for paychecks and start collecting them while living your life. And before long, you reach the point where you can honestly say, "Boy, these dividends are making me thirsty."

data.title: Why Future S&P 500 Returns May Disappoint - And How I'm Positioning Now
data.description:
Introduction

We often discuss inflation, as it’s somewhat of a silent killer of wealth. While a $100 bill will still be a $100 bill a few years from now, it tends to buy less over time. Since the early 2000s, the purchasing power of the dollar has lost roughly half of its value. Personally, I believe that number is higher, but using official numbers (see below), it’s roughly 50%.

Now, don’t get me wrong. This doesn’t mean life gets worse over time. $1,000 in the early 1910s got you goods worth less than $80 in the 1990s. However, I think we can all agree that life in 1990s America was better than in the 1910s.

As negative as inflation may be, consistent wage hikes and innovation tend to improve life over time. However, there are certain periods when inflation is an issue. Currently, for example, I think it’s fair to say that younger people are in a tricky spot due to factors like high home prices, elevated mortgage rates, and AI disruption in many white-collar jobs.

Furthermore, tough times can result in serious wealth challenges.

That’s what this article is about, as I think it’s one of the most serious articles I have written this year so far.

So, without further ado, let’s keep this intro short and get right to it!

We Are So Spoiled. Will It Last?

I started investing in 2011. While that is nothing compared to the track record that some of you may have, there are many on Wall Street who joined after me. And in general, younger generations have seen some of the best years on Wall Street. Since 2011, for example, we have been through two bear markets, one in 2020 and one in 2022.

I’m convinced that if I had started making good money 1-2 years earlier, I would have achieved all of my financial targets before turning 30. But it is what it is. While I cannot complain in my situation, we need to play the cards we’re dealt.

That brings me to the most important chart of this article. Below, you see the inflation-adjusted S&P 500. It’s simply the ratio between the S&P 500 and CPI inflation.

It’s a nice chart that tells us that any long-term investor would have made a lot of money over time by simply doing nothing, except for consistently buying the index.

That’s the good news.

The bad news is that, over time, the market has been through prolonged periods without any real gains (real means adjusted for inflation). For example, between the mid-1960s and the early 1990s (that’s roughly 25 years), the S&P 500 had no positive real return.

Between the early 2000s and 2014, it happened again. Investors saw 14 years without a net gain. Moreover, if you had bought in 1955, the market would have tested your initial entry again in 1982. There are many such instances that tell us that the uptrend we have seen since 2009 (with some bumps along the way) isn’t guaranteed.

And that’s my point.

I’m 30 now. If we were to enter a period of >20 years without net gains (this is NOT a prediction), I would be 50+ by the time I enjoy real capital gains again. Between then and now, most of my wealth would come from new capital additions without the post-inflation capital appreciation tailwind.

Again, this isn’t a prediction. I’m just painting a dark picture here based on past events.

Why Risks Are Elevated

Everything I wrote so far, I could have written in any market, as I simply observed and gave you some facts from the past. What matters right now is that risks are rising.

For example, during the 1970s, which was in the middle of a horrible return period, as we just discussed, the market was quite expensive, although way less expensive than it is right now. That difference is mainly caused by factors like higher margins.

This ruined the party for investors. While earnings continued to grow (see the chart below), the stock market didn’t rise, as its valuation multiple went down. That more than offset earnings growth. Note that the chart below is from 2016, but for the purpose of this article, it works just fine, as we perfectly see the components of the market’s total return.

Essentially, this explains why, over time, a “high” P/E multiple indicates subdued longer-term returns, as the odds are rising that positive earnings growth is offset by multiple compression.

Think about it like this. If Company XYZ generates $10 in EPS and trades at 30x earnings, it has a $300 stock price. Now, it grows that EPS number to $12, yet the market believes it’s only worth 20x due to AI disruption (or any other factor). Now, it trades at $240 despite positive earnings growth. Adobe Inc. (ADBE) investors have found this out the hard way recently.

To make matters worse, inflation further pressured the market’s real return.

Currently, inflation risks are elevated. For example, 1-year inflation swaps are back at 3.2%, while breakeven rates, which are way more volatile, are above 5.0%. The real number will likely be somewhere in the middle if crude oil prices remain high and disruption in other areas like agriculture, chemicals, and natural gas keeps global prices at a high level.

Moreover, I see structural risks. As I have often written before, I believe in a “higher for longer” scenario until we get the full deflationary effect from AI. This inflation is fueled by factors like energy bottlenecks, deglobalization, and government policies. That’s the “run it hot” thesis, which is simply based on a likely path where the government will accept higher inflation for the sake of broadening economic growth and lowering the real value of the national debt.

If that plan works out, it could be great for the economy. Yet inflation is a major risk.

Here are some thoughts on this from Main Street Alpha, an upcoming Investing Group service on Seeking Alpha I'm involved with that is launching soon:

Therefore, the monetary and fiscal authorities are quietly aligned in letting inflation run hot. It is a stealth default. They are inflating away the debt burden while publicly discussing the rising cost of living for obvious political purposes. This creates an environment where running it hot using inflation becomes an unofficial policy.

We are transitioning from a globalist, low-inflation environment, where tech multiples could expand to the moon on the back of zero-interest-rate policy, to a nationalist, high-nominal-growth era. - Main Street Alpha

Moreover, this is what we wrote regarding the market rotation, which is entirely fueled by this economic transition, so to speak:

[...] they are rotating aggressively into "deep value" plays. The logic here is incredibly simple, yet widely misunderstood by retail investors: if the regime is shifting toward nominal growth and persistent inflation, the biggest winners won't be the innovators. The biggest winners will be the indebted, the asset-heavy, and the monopolistic. - Main Street Alpha

That would be an issue, as the market is massively overweight in tech stocks.

Generally speaking, when the market is heavily concentrated, it tends to risk subdued returns, as any rotation would mean that the “rest of the market” would have to do the heavy lifting. Regardless of the type of rotation we’re talking about, this tends to hurt future returns.

In the 1960s and 1970s, that was the case as well. Furthermore, back then, inflation was a risk.

Today’s market is very different from the 1960s and 1970s, yet there are key macro similarities.

Now What?

As I said, I wish I were a few years older (all else being equal) so I could have started my career a bit earlier. But it is what it is. I’m not bearish on the S&P 500 in the sense that I expect the index to drop, but I am convinced that the returns of the next few years will not be as rosy as in the past decade.

In fact, I think the odds are against us, meaning we could see a prolonged period of subdued returns.

Does that mean I’m worried? No. Absolutely not.

There are plenty of opportunities if we simply look under the market’s hood or in other areas. That’s the good thing about a top-heavy market, as there tends to be great value behind the stocks that get all the attention and are in the market’s driver's seat.

Gold is one of them. In the 1970s, it was one of the best assets to own. While one factor is that it became more accessible, it also benefited from inflation and subdued equity returns.

I own no gold and no miners, yet I hope to change that this year.

Presently, gold is weak, which may be surprising in light of inflation and war risks. However, this is what we call the “scarcity plot.” Basically, because of the war, the U.S. is dominating supply chains like energy, agriculture, and capital. It’s becoming a bit of an oasis in the global macro landscape. That’s bullish for the dollar and temporarily bad for gold.

I’m paraphrasing and cutting it short, but that’s basically the gist of it.

It’s also why we are looking to buy gold (miners) on weakness for the next few years.

Moreover, I stick to my (cyclical) value thesis.

As I wrote in an article titled “Forget FANG - I’m Betting On ‘TOLL’ Stocks Instead.”

I explained why I believe hard asset stocks will drive the market going forward, mainly due to their ability to offset inflation risks, benefit from broadening growth, and, in some cases, be at the receiving end of trillions in long-term AI spending.

TOLL stands for:

  • Tangible assets (companies that are hard to disrupt by AI).
  • Oligopoly (limited competition).
  • Low incremental capital intensity (the ability to scale margins based on past CapEx investments). Think of a railroad that has all tracks in place and can now grow margins.
  • Long-duration cash flows (somewhat predictable long-term cash flows).

This includes industrial stocks. I think the State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) and many of its holdings will beat the S&P 500 for many years to come.

The same goes for basic materials, energy, and real estate, just to name a few.

And even in the tech space, I think some will do just fine. One of my favorites is Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), which I will give a lot more attention going forward, as I promised.

Moreover, while I don’t have the exact data to back it up, knowing who my reader base is, I think most of us who own quality dividend (growth) stocks, cyclical value like the ones I just mentioned, and precious metals should be in a good spot to generate “decent” longer-term returns.

The biggest risk, in my view, lies in being overly dependent on the S&P 500 or Big Tech/software. I may be wrong, but unless inflation comes crashing down and the growth rebound remains a thesis, we’ll likely end up seeing subdued S&P 500 returns for a prolonged period.

Takeaway

I don’t write this stuff to scare anyone. Our time is way too valuable for that.

Articles like this one are to prepare investors and explain the shifts in risk/reward I see for major assets, in this case, the S&P 500 and some of its biggest holdings.

The way I see it is that inflation is likely to become a bigger headwind for purchasing power and market valuations, which, historically speaking, is not a great sign for what’s to come.

That’s why I am advocating for a bigger rotation into hard assets, TOLL-like stocks, high-quality dividend growers, and assets like gold. If you own any of these, you’ll likely be fine.

I think the biggest risk is the consensus of the past few years.

data.title: Columbia Small Cap Value And Inflection Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Our philosophy

Simply put, the upward inflection in stock prices can be a confirmatory signal of fundamental improvement. Our approach to investing is based on our view that fundamentals ultimately drive stock prices. Through deep fundamental research, we aim to exploit market anomalies to identify stocks that should outperform the market. Once we identify an opportunity, the signal of upward inflection in price provides the impetus to invest. We believe that investing in companies that demonstrate an ability to compound capital over time, maintain strong fundamental attributes and trade at discounts to intrinsic value will lead to durable outperformance over a full market cycle. Our work relies on proprietary research and our experience investing over many different market cycles. We believe that the integrity and intensity of our research process leads to success. Our clients benefit from in-depth internally generated research from Columbia Threadneedle Investments' outstanding fundamental analyst team, quantitative research department and big-data team. Our passion for investing and deep belief in our philosophy are foundational to our strategy, which we are proud to offer our clients. This philosophy has been employed on behalf of our clients for years, and our north star (our philosophy) will continue to guide us forward for years to come.

Performance

Columbia Small Cap Value and Inflection Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Value Index, during the fourth quarter of 2025. Stock selection was the primary detractor, and sector allocation decisions also detracted from performance during the quarter. Stock selection in the industrials sector proved most challenging, followed by meaningful headwinds from stock picks in health care, consumer discretionary and communication services. Selection in financials provided the strongest positive offset, with utilities also contributing favorably. Sector allocation detracted modestly, particularly due to an underweight position in communication services, a sector that outperformed during this quarter. The strategy's fundamental value approach encountered relative headwinds as market leadership shifted toward more speculative factors, including stocks with negative profitability. While the quarter delivered disappointing relative performance, our confidence in this time-tested strategy remains strong. We believe current headwinds will dissipate as our fundamental analysis — combining attractive valuations with strong momentum characteristics — should perform well for clients over the long term.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Quarterly conversations with companies

The U.S. economy is experiencing a rare and dangerous phenomenon: simultaneous peak profitability in financial services and structural demand destruction in the real economy. This isn't a normal cycle divergence — it's a policy-induced dislocation, in which today's winners may become tomorrow's casualties.

Regional banks are reporting their best margins in years, while some of their customers are struggling. Net interest margins (NIMs) expanded as deposit costs fell faster and loan yields repriced positively. This created a profitability surge that management teams acknowledge may be peaking as variable loans reprice downward and deposit competition intensifies. Credit quality is bifurcating violently beneath these headline numbers. Some Midwest community banks report pristine portfolios with zero nonperforming assets, while other markets show non-performing assets increasing substantially. The divergence isn't random — it maps to commercial-real-estate concentration, underwriting discipline and exposure to government-dependent sectors. More critically, provisions for credit losses at subprime auto lenders are nearly double the pre-pandemic rate, while pawn receivables have grown by double digits, highlighting the difficulties lower-income consumers face as they exhaust liquidity buffers. The credit cycle is turning, but peak NIM is obscuring it. Banks trading on current earnings multiples may be mispriced. The question isn't whether margins will compress, but whether credit costs will accelerate faster than consensus expects when the NIM tailwind reverses. We will be monitoring first quarter 2026 provision expenses closely, and we are currently significantly underweight financials and banks specifically.

Simultaneously, tariff restructuring is forcing permanent supply-chain reconfiguration that separates winners from losers. Companies reducing China-sourced content are facing margin compression that won't be easy to recover, as these are permanent structural costs. Conversely, companies with domestic manufacturing footprints are maintaining pricing power and gaining market share: North American manufacturing enables them to take "nominal pricing in single digits" while competitors implement "double digit" increases and lose volume. This isn't temporary competitive advantage — it's a potentially permanent market-share transfer. The tariff environment is accelerating consolidation toward domestic producers while forcing import-dependent competitors into margin-destroying restructuring.

The unifying insight: we're in a rare moment where backward-looking metrics (peak bank margins, strong employment, resilient consumer spending) obscure forward-looking deterioration (credit stress, government retrenchment, permanent supply-chain costs). In our view, investors positioned for continuity will be blindsided. Those recognizing the inflection can reposition before consensus catches up.

Voice of America survey

Small companies are the explicit engine of American exceptionalism. They invest in America through their spending of capital to expand capacity, hire our neighbors as they grow and promote local community growth through increased tax revenue. In short, American small businesses are the lifeblood of the economy. With that in mind, many of the names we invest in are not household names. We describe them as being the company headquartered in the office park on the side of the highway you drive home on each night. Many Wall Street firms lack the resources to research these investments, and we are honored to be able to invest in such a dynamic and inefficient asset class for our clients.

A value-add component to this commentary is based on a poll we did of small companies in December 2025 — our Voice of America Survey. Given that this is our second year conducting this survey, data are only from this period. We will continue to report quarterly how small businesses in America are looking at the country around them.

The summary from our fourth-quarter survey shows that participants remain optimistic about growth prospects, with revenue expectations remaining at an elevated level relative to the past two years. These companies are balanced in expectations of raising their prices. Promotional activity has remained stable across all periods, suggesting businesses are maintaining consistent customer engagement strategies without feeling pressured to increase discounting significantly.

From a cost perspective, input-price pressures have remained elevated, and last year's baseline inflation appears to be stubborn. Labor costs continue to present challenges, with expectations holding steady but remaining above historical norms, indicating that wage pressures remain "sticky." Tax considerations have become slightly favorable, suggesting companies will potentially be benefiting from tax relief or credits compared to recent periods.

The local business environment presents a mixed picture. While restaurants and local establishments remain reasonably busy, this represents a softening from the more vibrant activity levels seen in previous quarters. More concerning is the dramatic resurgence of cost-of-living pressures, which have jumped back to match last year's elevated levels after moderating last quarter — indicating that inflation in housing, utilities and food costs has returned to concerning heights in participants' communities. Approximately 38% of the respondents have seen significant infrastructure upgrades in their local communities, a noticeable reduction from last quarter's observation.

Outlook

We continue to helm the Columbia Small Cap Value and Inflection Fund with an eye for opportunity. Small-cap value, as an asset class, represents "American exceptionalism," as the companies in our universe generate a high proportion of their revenue domestically. data.title: Trinity Capital: A 14.3% Yield That Investors Don't Need To Fear
data.description:
Introduction

With the last few years centered around the direction of inflation, the demand for higher-yield investments has seemed to grow. Covered call funds have emerged in popularity. But one sector that I've been a fan of for years is the BDC (BIZD) sector.

Once seen as riskier investments due to their lending to private, sometimes financially distressed businesses, many investors scoffed at the thought of owning BDCs as long-term investments.

And the sector has been in the spotlight recently due to the SaaSpocalypse: the threat of AI causing disruption in software businesses. For BDCs, many have high exposure to Software, which has resulted in higher volatility.

The company I discuss today, Trinity Capital (TRIN), also has exposure to the sector, but minimal compared to some peers. Lower interest rates have also weighed on the sector.

Usually, when dividend stocks have double-digit yields, this can be an indication of lower quality, putting them at higher risk of a dividend cut.

While a lower share price has pushed TRIN's yield above 14%, I don't believe their dividend is in any danger of being cut in the near term.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, why this BDC has made its way into my top 5 favorites, and why I believe their dividend will remain safe in 2026.

Previous Thesis

It's been well over a year since I last covered Trinity Capital, rating the stock a hold due to the threat of rate cuts causing underperformance. Due to a lack of catalysts, I believed TRIN and the entire sector could potentially see downside despite their attractive yields.

Since TRIN is down close to 6% at the time of writing. This is compared to the S&P (SP500), up close to 5%. Despite the lack of catalysts, TRIN performed well and saw record numbers, resulting in AUM reaching $2 billion. Dividend coverage was solid at 106% with additional liquidity of $64.5 million to temporarily cover shortfalls.

Why TRIN Moved Into My Top 5 Favorite BDCs

While many of my picks have remained the same, Trinity Capital has made its way onto the list, seeing how they've navigated the challenging macro environment.

But I'll save my list for another article.

In their latest quarter last month, TRIN again showed why they're a quality BDC. And their premium to NAV speaks to this. Especially considering many have sold off hard in the past 3 months or so.

For context, below is a chart of TRIN's performance against some of its peers year-to-date. You can see significantly larger peers like Ares Capital (ARCC), FSK KKR Capital (FSK), and Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) have all experienced a steeper sell-off.

Part of this has to do with their exposure to the software sector. ARCC's portfolio has 24% exposure, while FSK's is 16.4%. Software is currently their largest sector, while OBDC's exposure is more reasonable at 11.1%. TRIN's sits at just 9.2%, which may also be a factor in them performing better.

During their Q4, net investment income was flat from the prior quarter at $0.52. This did decline from $0.58 in the previous year's quarter due to lower interest rates. But I, personally, like to look for growth on a total basis, as per-share declines may not tell the story of a BDC's quality.

Their bottom line on a total basis actually grew from approximately $34.6 million to $39.9 million year-over-year. For the year, this reached a record $144 million, or $2.08 a share. This led to a 17.5% increase in their top line to $83 million.

Commitments reached a record $2.1 billion and funding $1.5 billion, a 21% increase from the prior year. For the quarter, investment commitments were $543.1 million, while fundings were $435 million.

This resulted in strong portfolio growth as their value increased from $1.7 billion to $2.4 billion. Another way I like to assess a BDC's quality is if they can see NAV growth over time, usually over a 1-year period or longer.

Even if their NAV declines, this should be minimal, as steeper ones could suggest lower credit quality. TRIN's NAV grew from $13.35 to $13.42.

Their internally managed structure also gives them an advantage over several peers. I like to look at them as a mini Capital Southwest (CSWC) or mini Main Street Capital (MAIN).

Another reason for TRIN's outperformance is their superior loan structure. Unlike many BDCs, many of TRIN's debt investments have a floor. Meaning they see less of an impact from interest rate cuts in comparison due to how their loans are structured.

Speaking with TRIN's CEO a few years ago, this is something he emphasized when I asked how they planned to navigate a lower rate environment.

This is why their NII only declined $0.06 on a per-share basis and showed more stability through 2025. This is in comparison to someone like FSK or even Blue Owl Capital Corp.'s.

FSK saw their bottom line decline $0.14 from $0.66 to $0.52 in their latest quarter. OBDC's NII fell $0.11 from $0.47 to $0.36 a share.

TRIN's management spoke to this during earnings, mentioning further rate cuts likely having little impact.

Moving to rate cuts. So far, they've had minimal impact on our business. Based on our modeling, additional cuts would likely have a muted effect on our earnings power. Unlike most other lenders, the majority of our loans have interest rate floors set at or near the original levels. So, when rates fall, our income does not fall proportionately.

While TRIN's CEO spoke about their loan structure on a call with me a few years ago, seeing how they've actually navigated rate cuts and a lower rate environment is impressive.

Especially considering many larger BDCs have seen their finances deteriorate, leading to dividend cuts and underperformance. Speaking of dividend cuts, TRIN also moved to a monthly pay schedule, following in the footsteps of CSWC, who did this back in 2025.

Another testament to their quality is their low non-accruals. These accounted for less than 1% of the portfolio on a fair value basis. During the quarter, 2 smaller borrowers were added, while two were removed. At quarter's end, 4 borrowers were on non-accrual status.

14.3% Yield Is Likely Safe

Regarding their dividend, TRIN moved to a monthly pay schedule, following in the footsteps of CSWC, who did this back in 2025. I believe this will only bode well for TRIN for the foreseeable future.

And although macro uncertainty remains high, I believe Trinity Capital is one of the BDCs that can maintain its dividend for 2026. They also had $0.84, or $69 million, in undistributed taxable income they could use for shortfalls.

While I don't see them increasing the dividend in the near future, I also believe they will be able to maintain their current one. I also wouldn't be surprised if they rewarded shareholders with an end-of-year supplemental or special.

In the near future, I believe we will see more BDCs forced to reduce their dividends due to deteriorating borrower fundamentals and potential rate cuts. There have been talks about the credibility of private loans.

And with the challenging economic backdrop likely to continue for the near term, many BDCs are at higher risk. Particularly if we end up seeing rate cuts in the back half of the year.

Balance Sheet

TRIN's balance sheet was in good shape with manageable debt maturities in the coming years. Debt-to-equity was well below the sector median's 1.25x at 1.18x.

Their premium to NAV also gives them an additional lever to raise capital to satisfy upcoming debt maturities or keep the dividend intact should coverage tighten. They can also use this capital for new investment commitments to grow the portfolio and bottom line.

Valuation and Risks

Although Wall Street sees upside with a price target of $16.61, I don't see any catalysts that could push the share price higher. Moreover, the ongoing war with Iran will likely result in heightened uncertainty, especially if it continues for much longer.

If it's resolved soon, then this could put rate cuts back on the table as well. These are reasons I remain cautious on the sector as a whole and reiterate my hold rating.

But if you're someone strictly looking for income, then Trinity Capital is one of the highest quality yields, in my opinion. And it is one of the few BDCs I believe can maintain their dividend amidst macro uncertainty.

Conclusion

In recent years, Trinity Capital has risen to one of my top 5 favorite BDCs with how they've navigated the challenging economic landscape.

Their superior loan structure speaks to their astute management, as well as their premium to NAV. Furthermore, their superior loan structure has allowed them to perform well, while many peers have been forced to reduce their dividends, leading to higher underperformance.

While a lack of catalysts and the potential to see a longer war keep me cautious, as this could lead to a recession, Trinity Capital's high dividend yield is a case of overall market sentiment and not a case of their credit quality. Moreover, I believe their premium to NAV further indicates this, and this is why I believe TRIN's 14.3% yield will remain safe.

Contributors

Detractors

Stock selection in the health care sector was the largest contributor to relative performance, notably in the biotechnology industry. Security selection in the IT sector, namely in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry, boosted relative results. In the financials sector, selection decisions in the financial services industry added value.

 The largest detractor was stock selection in the consumer discretionary sector, notably in the hotels, restaurants & leisure industry. In the real estate sector, security selection in the real estate management & development industry hindered performance. Another meaningful detractor included security selection in the industrials sector, particularly in the aerospace & defense industry.

U.S. equities enter 2026 with strong momentum after another standout year. Despite questions about enthusiasm driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and global performance dispersion, the macroeconomic backdrop remains supportive, with Federal Reserve interest rate cuts, resilient consumers, upgraded growth forecasts, and policy tailwinds boosting competitiveness. Structural themes – AI, health care innovation, defense technology, “Gen Z” (typically people born in the United States between the late 1990s and early 2010s) consumer trends, and digitization – continue to shape long-term growth. data.title: Commodities: Oil Eases As Report Suggests War Could End Without Hormuz Reopening
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist and Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Oil edged lower Tuesday morning after the Wall Street Journal reported that US President Donald Trump told aides he would be willing to end the military campaign in Iran even if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed

Energy – Oil eases as report suggests war could end without Hormuz reopening

Oil edged lower Tuesday morning after the Wall Street Journal reported that US President Donald Trump told aides he would be willing to end the military campaign in Iran even if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. The pullback came despite another Iranian attack on a tanker in the Persian Gulf, with Brent trading around $107 a barrel in early trade.

On Monday, President Trump said a deal to end hostilities remains possible. He also threatened potential attacks on Iran’s energy infrastructure, including the Kharg Island export terminal. Despite pulling back, Brent is on track for a record monthly gain, with prices up around 60% in March. The US benchmark is up more than 50% in March after closing yesterday above $100 a barrel for the first time since July 2022.

Crude opened the week higher after Iran‑backed Houthi militants in Yemen entered the conflict and additional US troops arrived in the region, raising concerns over shipping disruptions. The risk to the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait – a critical chokepoint linking the Red Sea to global markets – has resurfaced, with the Houthis having previously shut the Red Sea to most Western shippers after war in Gaza began in 2023.

Meanwhile, Iran has moved to formalise its control over the Strait of Hormuz, restricting vessel movements while allowing limited traffic, including ships from Pakistan, Thailand and Malaysia. Two state-owned Chinese container ships were also trying to exit Hormuz on Monday. A toll or selective access through Hormuz would keep a persistent risk premium in oil, as flows could be curtailed at short notice, while higher insurance and freight costs lift delivery prices even without a full shutdown.

In refined products, US retail gasoline prices are hovering around $4 per gallon, according to the American Automobile Association. A sustained move above this level would mark the first breach of this psychological threshold since 2022, adding pressure on both household and business fuel costs.

Metals – Aluminium rallies on Middle East supply risks

Aluminium prices rallied on Monday, briefly nearing $3,500/t on the LME, as Middle East supply risks escalated. Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) said it sustained significant damage at its Abu Dhabi smelter, while Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) is assessing the impact at its facility after Iran’s Revolutionary Guard said the sites were targeted in retaliation for US‑Israeli strikes. Together, the two smelters account for around 3.2Mt of annual capacity, and any prolonged outage would further tighten an already constrained market, where restarting smelters is costly, complex, and time-consuming.

Both companies have yet to provide details on the extent of the damage.

Aluminium is on track for a monthly gain of 10%.

The escalation comes on top of already tightening supply conditions across the Gulf. Recent curtailments at Alba and reduced operations at Qatalum have already affected around 560kt of annual capacity, equivalent to roughly 8-9% of regional supply.

The Middle East produces around 6-6.5Mt of aluminium per year, accounting for roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply. Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) smelters are highly export‑oriented, with a large share of output sold into international markets rather than consumed domestically.

Agriculture – White sugar rises on supply disruptions

White sugar prices briefly hit their highest level since October 2025 before easing by the close. Escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are tightening supply, as Gulf refiners rely heavily on steady raw sugar inflows from Brazil and other key exporters. Shipping delays and cargo diversions have created supply gaps across the Middle East, East Africa and parts of Asia. With the conflict now in its fifth week and no clear signs of de‑escalation, risks to traffic through the Strait of Hormuz have increased, further constraining flows. The white‑to‑raw sugar premium rose to $109.4/t, from around $92/t before the conflict.

CBOT soybean futures also advanced, while soybean oil rebounded toward its strongest level since 2022. Higher crude prices are supporting demand for biofuel feedstocks, while disruptions to fertiliser and fuel shipments linked to the Strait of Hormuz are adding upward pressure on grain and edible oil prices. Additional support came from the Trump administration’s newly released biofuel blending rules, which raise mandated usage versus last year’s proposal and bolster demand for biofuel‑linked crops.

In Europe, the European Commission may convene an urgent stakeholder meeting on 13 April to discuss near‑term and structural fertiliser measures. An action plan, expected shortly, aims to lift EU fertiliser production, cut import dependency, improve on‑farm nutrient efficiency and accelerate the shift toward bio‑based and low‑carbon alternatives.

data.title: Invesco Global Real Estate Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global macroeconomic conditions during the quarter were characterized by monetary easing, though inflationary pressures persisted across major economies. The US Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate in October in response to labor market weakness, though inflation remained above target. The European Central Bank approached the end of its interest rate cutting cycle, while the outlook for UK interest rates remained uncertain amid ongoing policy adjustments. Japan's economy grew above trend, with expectations for additional monetary tightening, in contrast to generally accommodative stances elsewhere. Against this backdrop, global listed real estate securities declined for the quarter.
  • Global listed real estate equities ended the quarter trading even with their average underlying net asset value. However, this statistic hid wide dispersions in the valuations of countries and property types.
  • Our outlook for global listed real estate remains cautiously constructive as monetary policy has remained accommodative in major economies. Conditions should be most supportive for markets and sectors that have already experienced valuation declines and where tenant demand can provide a structural tailwind. This is especially true for landlords supplying high quality assets into supply-constrained or higher demand markets and thus commanding pricing power.

Portfolio positioning

The fund has investments in real estate common equities, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and REIT preferred shares and is diversified across all global regions. At quarter end, the fund's allocation was 85% real estate equities, 10% CMBS, 1% preferred securities, 0% corporate bonds and 4% cash.

Relative to the fund's average positioning in the past, it now has greater exposure to real estate common equities, based on our view that over the medium term, lower interest rates are likely to reward real estate equities with higher returns than the relatively more stable real estate fixed income investments.

The fund's real estate fixed income holdings are focused on single asset and/or single borrower investments, with residential and lodging representing the largest property type exposures. To limit the fixed income duration, CMBS investments are largely floating rate or relatively short duration fixed rate positions.

Real estate common equity exposures currently emphasize data centers, industrial and senior living assets, where growth opportunity appears promising and valuations look attractive to us. The most significant underweights are in retail, office and residential REITs, where fundamental trends and valuation levels suggest to us modest relative value opportunities in a changing economic environment.

During the quarter, the fund's allocation to real estate common equity rose and the allocation to real estate fixed income declined. Compared to the previous quarter end, the fund's allocations to health care, data center and industrial REITs increased, reflecting strong fundamentals and secular demand drivers. Exposures to specialty, residential and office REITs decreased. We increased exposure in the Asia Pacific region during the quarter. Exposure in Continental Europe and the UK also increased, while US exposure declined.

Performance highlights

The fund outperformed its equity-only benchmark, the FTSE EPRA Nareit Developed Index. The fund's holdings of CMBS and stock selection in the Americas were the largest contributors to relative return.

Contributors to performance

In a period when global listed real estate had a negative absolute return, the fund's allocation to high quality fixed income securities and specifically CMBS generated positive absolute returns and helped to offset weaker returns from real estate equity.

In the Americas, the US was the primary contributor to relative return. Within the US, an overweight in senior housing REIT American Healthcare REIT added to relative return as management raised earnings guidance and announced the completion of almost $1 billion in acquisitions in 2025. The fund benefited from an underweight in Alexandria Real Estate Equities, which experienced challenges including worse-than-expected earnings, a reduction in its quarterly dividend payment and deteriorating fundamentals. The US industrial sector also added to relative return, benefiting from rising market rents and company-specific catalysts. An underweight in retail was advantageous as the sector declined.

In the UK, an underweight in Unite Group, a student housing company in the residential sector, was one of the largest contributors to relative return. Unite Group suffered a decline after disappointing leasing updates amid political and economic uncertainties.

Detractors from performance

In the Asia Pacific region, an overweight in Australia's data base sector detracted from absolute and relative returns as the sector declined due to refinancing risk and higher effective borrowing costs that weighed on valuations. Link REIT, which owns and operates a diversified portfolio of retail, office and logistics real estate in Hong Kong, detracted from relative return, as did allocations to data centers in Singapore. In the Europe, Middle East and Africa segment, underweights in Israel and Switzerland detracted from relative return.

Iron Mountain, a US specialty REIT, was one of the largest individual detractors from relative return. The company's document storage business has performed well, but the leasing of data center development projects fell short of expectations during the quarter. We have seen that this key source of future growth can be volatile on a quarterly basis. We maintain the fund's overweight given the company's robust growth profile and what we see as attractive relative valuation. An underweight in Ventas, a US senior housing REIT, also detracted as the sector continued to benefit from a recovery in senior housing fundamentals since COVID. We prefer to source senior housing exposure from an overweight in American Healthcare REIT, which we believe is superior given the company's external growth that may boost earnings.

data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) suffers historic monthly rout, but Goldman Sachs doubles down on $5,400 target. (00:15) GM temporarily lays off 1,300 workers at Detroit EV plant. (01:14) Netflix (NFLX) eyes bigger NFL package with more live games -- report. (01:49)

This is an abridged transcript.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is ticking higher. Spot gold is up about 1% to $4,567 per ounce but down 14% this month. Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) advanced 4.4% to $73 but slipped 23% so far this month.

The 14% fall so far this month puts it on track for its steepest fall since October 2008, weighed down by a stronger dollar index (DXY) and fading expectations of a U.S. interest rate cut this ​year. Prices are, however, ​up about 5% for ⁠the quarter.

Goldman Sachs retains its bullish view on gold despite the recent selloff, forecasting renewed gains by the end of 2026.

Gold’s medium-term outlook remains intact, and the precious metal may reach $5,400 an ounce, analysts Lina Thomas and Daan Struyven said in a note, citing continued central bank purchases and two more US rate cuts this year, according to media reports.

General Motors (GM) is halting operations at Factory ZERO until April 13, 2026. This extends downtime that initially started around March 16.

The automaker said the move is meant to align EV output with current market demand after a roughly 50% cut in production at Factory ZERO earlier in 2026.

This underscores a broader slowdown in EV adoption in the U.S. GM has also taken significant financial hits tied to its EV strategy, including writedowns totaling about $7.6B.

Netflix (NFLX) is reportedly looking to double its coverage of National Football League games. The streamer is seeking a four-game package that would add a game on the day before Thanksgiving as well as an international game.

Under its current package with the NFL expiring at the end of 2026, Netflix (NFLX) has a three-year deal for a specialized Christmas Day package, which typically includes two games.

According to The Wall Street Journal, the NFL is taking a “flexible approach” to selling additional games and could open up negotiations with other current rights holders, including Google (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN).

Unilever, McCormick near merger to create global food giant

  • New Coca-Cola (KO) CEO Henrique Braun will take over the top spot from James Quincey, who will transition to the executive chairman position.

  • Royal Gold (RGLD) will hold an Investor Day event.

  • Nike (NKE) will hold its earnings conference call. Options trading on Nike implies a 9% post-earnings move. The update from management on trends in China will also be closely watched by other consumer companies with a higher exposure to the nation, including Starbucks (SBUX), Estee Lauder (EL), and Skechers (SKX), while On Holdings (ONON) has the closest trading correlation to Nike over the last year.

Stock index futures are sharply higher after the Wall Street Journal reported that President Trump told aides he is willing to end hostilities with Iran on a four-to-six-week timeline, prioritizing the degradation of Iran’s navy and missile stocks over the more complex mission of reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1% at $103. Bitcoin is down 0.5% at $66,000.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.7% and the DAX is up 0.8%. India (SENSEX) markets closed for a holiday.

data.title: National Vision Holdings: Growth Outlook Got Better
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE) previously with a buy rating as the turnaround story progressed in the right direction, and I expected further upside given the growth catalysts in place for 2H25. My view today is even more bullish. My reason is simple. Managed care is working, premiumization is extending beyond frames, and remote hybrid exams could help ease a real capacity bottleneck.

The customer mix is getting better

It has been ~7 months since my last update, and I think the biggest change is that EYE is no longer a turnaround story that still needs proof. For those who do not follow, the FY2025 results indicate that EYE is actually growing very healthily (not what you would expect from a business that is still deep in a turnaround phase). Net revenue from continuing operations grew 9% to $1.99 billion; adjusted comparable store sales grew 6%, and adjusted EBIT margin improved by 160 bps y/y to 5.2%. This is not a case where the topline accelerated, but earnings dragged. Both moved in the right direction.

More importantly, it is where these improvements came from. Back then, my view was that managed care would grow into a major growth driver, and indeed, that came true. Managed care comp sales grew low double-digits in FY2025, and management even said they ended the year with 42% of revenue attributable to managed care. Yes, total traffic for the year did come down 0.5%, but that was mostly due to a fall in self-pay customers, which doesn't matter since EYE gained traffic in more profitable cohorts. Then, if you combine this with the fact that progressive and outside Rx customers, on top of managed care, were up high-single digits on comp, with traffic for those customers up mid-single digits, it is clear that EYE is winning in the parts of the business that matter most. In other words, the quality of growth is improving. I would say this is a much better setup than relying on heavy discounting to drive volume from lower-value traffic. The current strategy works much better for the long term since better-quality customers usually mean better tickets (they spend more), better repeat behavior, and better margins.

Premiumization is no longer just about frames

The second big change is that EYE's premiumization strategy is no longer just about frames. For what it's worth, frames are still a big focus here, and EYE continues to do well. They ended FY2025 with ~40% of frames priced >$99, up from 20% at the start of the year. This change in mix appears to be well received by customers, as management also said some newer branded products were turning above market benchmarks. That tells me the improvement in customer mix (the point above) is real.

Aside from frames, the next leg of premiumization growth will be lenses, specifically add-ons like anti-reflective coating, advanced materials, transitions, and progressives (which all saw positive demand in FY2025). The plan for 2026 is to do what EYE already did on frames. For instance, include more premium lenses in the assortment, push for better pricing, and also simplify the assortment; launch a new tier (i.e., the Tier 4 progressive lens). EYE is also going to roll out Stellest, a premium product. The goal is to push premium materials penetration to ~60% from 50% today and the anti-reflective mix to ~50% from ~40% today.

The core assumption here is that consumers would buy these premium lenses, which I think they will. The way I see it, premium lenses are usually easier to sell because there is a clear functional benefit, not just a nicer look (like frames). Things like anti-reflective coating, better materials, and progressive lenses all improve day-to-day use, and consumers can feel it in real time (which gives them a clearer reason to trade up). The impact on EYE P&L is that this should lift the average ticket, which should flow through to better margins.

Remote hybrid exam

One new catalyst that I figured out is the remote hybrid exam model. In the US, exam capacity is one of the real constraints in optical retail. The lack of exam capacity also means weaker customer conversion since the customer wouldn't know what lens degree to purchase. With exam capacity, EYE can improve marketing, upgrade assortment, and bring in better customers, but that doesn't work if the stores cannot support exam demand consistently. That is why I think this remote model matters. With the remote hybrid exam model, it basically eases the constraint since doctors can be “utilized” across multiple stores without being physically there. EYE is doubling down on this and already has remote telehealth capability installed in >800 locations. This should give EYE more flexibility in how it uses doctor capacity across the fleet and reduce the risk that demand gets lost simply because exam coverage is not there.

Valuation

We are back to the low end of EYE's valuation range (NTM P/E) over the past 3 years, which I think is a good entry point for investors who have missed the previous rally. If you look at the small chart in the left picture, you would see that historically, consensus estimates have been fairly accurate, if not underestimating actual performance. Current consensus estimates call for adjusted EPS to grow from $0.70 to $1.80 over 4 years, which is about ~27% CAGR. For a business that compounds adjusted EPS by high-20%, I think a 23x NTM P/E doesn't make sense since that is near where the current S&P 500 index trades at (19.5x NTM P/E with mid-teens adjusted EPS CAGR). EYE should trade at a decent premium given its growth profile, so if we assume EYE reverts back to the mean (28.5x), the stock is easily worth $51.

Risk

If the next leg of premiumization fails to work, i.e., attachment rates on higher-end lens products disappoint, the runway for higher ticket and margin expansion could look less optimistic than expected. The ramp-up in remote exam capacity would also add fixed costs to the business. If demand fails to come through as expected, margins could stay weak for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for EYE. Things have developed in the right direction. Managed care is driving up customer quality, premiumization now has a second leg in lenses, and remote hybrid exams could help unlock the next stage of growth by easing exam capacity constraints. At the same time, the stock is back near the low end of its recent NTM P/E range, which I think gives investors a reasonable entry point.
